payload.author: claude
payload.body:
Round 1 interview (bri as proxy for real collaborators).

BUYER SHAPE: 1:1 / micro-teams (2-4), globally remote, mixed skill — ranges from a senior AWS backend dev to an amateur Swift dev who can barely use git. NOT enterprise. No shared LAN ever; iOS app exists but barely used.

CORE PAIN (reframes the thesis): it is NOT merge correctness — git already unions events cleanly on pull. It is AWARENESS LATENCY. Moving a ticket to in_progress happens BEFORE making a branch/commit/pull, so the git channel structurally cannot carry it. Two remote people start the same task simultaneously because neither sees the other's live status. Defeats the 'birds-eye view of parallelized remote work' that is Scope's whole point.

FALLBACK TODAY: texting each other about who has what. No one pays for an alternative for this specific need.

IMPLICATION: the hosted relay's value prop is LIVE STATUS PRESENCE across remote collaborators (carrying the pre-commit changes git can't), not 'deterministic offline merge.' De-collision/merge stays as the durable floor, not the selling point. May also mean a LIGHTER architecture than full offline-first relay: a realtime presence/status channel layered over existing git sync, rather than a full cloud replica. To be pressure-tested in round 2 (pricing + willingness).
